---
title: "AI-Assisted Clinical Problem Solving"
subtitle: "A Multi-Persona Bayesian Diagnostic Reasoning Framework"
author: "Hsieh-Ting Lin"
institute: "Placeholder University Hospital, Taipei"
date: "November 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    width: 1600
    height: 900
    fontsize: 1.8em
    code-fold: true
    mermaid:
      theme: neutral
bibliography: ../shared/references.bib
---

## The Problem {.center}

::: {.callout-important}
Clinical diagnostic reasoning is **complex**, requiring multi-disciplinary expertise and systematic probability updating.
:::

- Diagnostic errors contribute to ~10% of patient deaths
- Traditional teaching: case-based, but lacks **quantitative** probability tracking
- How can AI help structure and teach diagnostic reasoning?

## Our Approach: CPS Framework

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 14
graph LR
    A["SCENARIO.md<br>(Patient Case)"] --> B["Symptom<br>Mapping"]
    B --> C["Round 1<br>Attending"]
    C --> D["Round 2<br>Radiology +<br>Pathology"]
    D --> E["Round 3<br>Subspecialist"]
    E --> F["Round 4<br>EBM Evidence"]
    F --> G["Round 5<br>Diagnostic<br>Conference"]
    G --> H["FINAL_DX.md"]

    style A fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#0288d1
    style H fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#388e3c
```

Models the **NEJM Clinical Problem-Solving** format with Bayesian LR reasoning

## Eight Medical Personas

| Persona | Round | Role | Activated When |
|---------|-------|------|----------------|
| **Attending (IM)** | 1 | H&P, problem representation, initial DDx | Always |
| **Radiologist** | 2 | Imaging interpretation with LRs | Imaging data present |
| **Pathologist** | 2 | Lab/biopsy interpretation with LRs | Lab data present |
| **Cardiologist** | 3 | ECG, echo, cath, risk scores | Chest pain, syncope |
| **Pulmonologist** | 3 | PFTs, ABG, chest CT | Dyspnea, wheezing |
| **Infectious Disease** | 3 | Cultures, serologies, travel DDx | Fever |
| **Neurologist** | 3 | Neuro exam, localization | Headache, AMS |
| **EBM Specialist** | 4 | Literature search, guidelines | Always |

## Bayesian LR Methodology

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

### The Formula

$$\text{Post-test odds} = \text{Pre-test odds} \times LR$$

$$\text{Probability} = \frac{\text{odds}}{1 + \text{odds}}$$

Each clinical finding **shifts** the probability via its likelihood ratio [@fagan1975; @mcgee2018].
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

### LR Interpretation

| LR+ | Effect |
|-----|--------|
| >10 | Strong rule-in |
| 5-10 | Moderate |
| 2-5 | Small |
| 1 | Useless |

| LR- | Effect |
|-----|--------|
| <0.1 | Strong rule-out |
| 0.1-0.2 | Moderate |
| 0.2-0.5 | Small |
:::
:::

## Case 1: 76F Dyspnea + Progressive Weakness

::: {.callout-note}
6-month progressive proximal weakness, bilateral ptosis, **areflexia**, intact sensation, normal CK, low CMAPs, prior SIADH, failed MG therapy
:::

**Problem representation**: 76F with progressive neuromuscular respiratory failure + areflexia + autonomic dysfunction

## Case 1: Bayesian Probability Cascade

| Finding | LR | Pre-test | Post-test |
|---------|-----|----------|-----------|
| Proximal weakness + areflexia | x5.0 | 35.0% | 72.9% |
| Low/absent CMAPs | x8.0 | 72.9% | 95.6% |
| SIADH (autonomic) | x3.0 | 95.6% | 98.5% |
| Failed MG therapy | x4.0 | 98.5% | **99.6%** |
| Heavy smoker (SCLC risk) | x2.5 | 99.6% | 99.8% |

::: {.fragment}
**Final Dx: Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome** (paraneoplastic, SCLC)

Correctly identified by Round 4 with transparent reasoning chain
:::

## Case 2: 38F Sudden Chest Pain

::: {.callout-note}
Mail carrier, **prior MI at age <38**, sudden substernal chest pain, 4 NTG tablets without relief
:::

The key question: **Why does a 38-year-old woman have a prior MI?**

## Case 2: Pivot 1-2

| Pivot | New Data | Leading Dx | Prob |
|-------|----------|-----------|------|
| **1** | Young F + prior MI | SCAD | 97.5% |
| **2** | Multi-territory MI + anemia | APS | 94.7% |

::: {.fragment}
Both seemed highly likely...

But both were **wrong**.
:::

## Case 2: Pivot 3 --- The Cath

Coronary angiography revealed:

- **LAD**: Aneurysmal, thrombosed at tortuous bend (proximal to prior stent)
- **LCx**: Diffusely aneurysmal
- **RCA**: Aneurysmal with partial occlusion

::: {.fragment}
**Diffuse 3-vessel coronary aneurysms** --- completely unexpected.

SCAD and APS **excluded**. New leading dx: Kawasaki Disease (90%)
:::

## Case 2: Pivot 4-5 --- The Skin Exam

Rheumatology noted subtle skin findings. Dermatology confirmed:

- **>6 cafe-au-lait spots** >1.5 cm
- **Axillary freckling** (Crowe sign)
- **Multiple neurofibromas**

::: {.fragment .highlight-red}
**3/7 NF1 diagnostic criteria met**

Final Dx: **Neurofibromatosis Type 1 Vasculopathy** (>95%)
:::

## The Key Insight

::: {.callout-warning}
## Bayesian Reasoning is Bounded by the Hypothesis Space

Post-test probability can **never exceed** the probability mass assigned to hypotheses in your differential.

If NF1 isn't in your Top 10, no amount of LR updating will find it.
:::

::: {.fragment}
This is the most important lesson for teaching differential diagnosis construction.
:::

## Safety Checks: Born from Failure

| Check | What It Catches | Derived From |
|-------|----------------|-------------|
| Red Flag History | Unusual PMH not investigated | Pivot 1 failure (Grade D) |
| Syndromic Screen | Skin/eye/CTD exam not done | Pivot 2 failure (Grade C+) |
| Rare Cause Search | Unexpected pattern not searched | Pivot 3 failure (Grade B-) |
| Hypothesis Space Audit | "Is there a Dx NOT in Top 10?" | Core Bayesian limitation |
| Genetic Pattern Recognition | AD family history not weighted | Pivot 4 (Grade B+) |
| Hypothesis Humility | DDx is never closed | Meta-principle |

## Validation Module

### Per-Phase Validation Checklist

```
Phase 1: Red Flag History Check (HARD GATE)
Phase 2: DDx completeness, MNM coverage, syndromic screen trigger
Phase 3: LR application (>80% target), rare cause search
Phase 4: Hypothesis Space Audit (MANDATORY after each round)
Phase 5: Bayesian convergence, MNM exclusion, unification test
```

### Retrospective Grading

Each phase graded A-F with specific failure modes. Safety checks evaluated: should-have-fired vs did-fire.

## Performance Metrics

| Metric | Case 1 (LEMS) | Case 2 (NF1) |
|--------|---------------|--------------|
| Diagnostic Accuracy | 1.0 (Correct) | 1.0 (Correct) |
| Pivot Efficiency | Round 1 | Round 5 |
| MNM Sensitivity | 100% | 100% |
| Hypothesis Coverage | Yes (Round 1) | No (Round 4) |
| LR Completeness | 85% | 78% |
| Overall Grade | A | B- |

## The Improvement Cycle

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 10
graph TD
    A[Run Case] --> B[Validate Each Phase]
    B --> C[Complete Case]
    C --> D[Retrospective Grading]
    D --> E{Failures Found?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Create Safety Check]
    F --> G[Update Skill]
    G --> A
    E -->|No| H[Update Performance Tracker]
    H --> A

    style F fill:#ffcdd2
    style G fill:#c8e6c9
```

## Conclusions

1. **Transparent reasoning**: Every probability shift cites a likelihood ratio with source

2. **Continuous improvement**: Case failures drive safety check development

3. **Pedagogical value**: The hypothesis space limitation is a teachable moment for DDx construction

4. **Multi-perspective**: 8 personas ensure no single viewpoint dominates

## Future Directions

- **Family Doctor Mode**: Leverage longitudinal patient data for earlier diagnosis
- **More test cases**: Stress-test with NEJM CPC cases across all 33 symptom categories
- **Performance benchmarking**: Track diagnostic accuracy and pivot efficiency over time
- **Integration with literature search**: Automated evidence retrieval via PubMed/Scopus APIs

## {.center}

::: {.r-fit-text}
Thank You
:::

**GitHub**: [github.com/htlin222/cps-skills](https://github.com/htlin222/cps-skills)

**Contact**: placeholder@example.com

::: {.callout-tip}
Scan QR code for the repository
:::

## References
